title: Why a newcomer smells a city before they see it
summary: Reflect on why an arriving outsider registers a city through its smell long before its skyline, and what the nose notices about a place that the eye is trained to overlook.
topic: Why an outsider experiences a new city first through smell rather than sight
prompt: Write a 450–600-word reflective essay on why a newcomer to a foreign city takes in its smell — diesel, spice, river, bread — before they take in its famous skyline, and what this says about how we truly come to know, and belong to, a place. Draw on two concrete sensory examples and concede one objection. Use cleft sentences, the passive voice, and complex concession (although / even though).
hintStarters: Long before I could name a single street, the city had already announced itself through…
Although the postcards sell the skyline, what actually lodges in memory is…
It is not the monument but the morning smell of a particular bakery that finally tells me…
Even though I told myself I was lost, my nose was quietly learning to…

hintVocab.meaningVi: thuộc về một nơi nào đó
thích nghi với môi trường mới
một địa danh / mốc dễ nhận
vẽ bản đồ một nơi trong đầu
ghi nhận / nhận biết điều gì
chất liệu của đời sống thường nhật
ổn định cuộc sống ở nơi mới
những con phố nhỏ phía sau
dần dần trở nên thân thuộc/được yêu thích
định hướng / tìm lại phương hướng

sampleText:
I arrived in the city at night, and for the first three days I could not have drawn its skyline if my life depended on it. Yet I could already have described its smell. Long before I could name a single street, the city had already announced itself through the nose: warm diesel near the station, a sweet rot of fruit by the canal, and, every morning at six, bread from a bakery I would not find for a week. The eye was overwhelmed and useless; the nose was already at work, sorting, comparing, and quietly deciding what kind of place this was.

There is a reason the nose gets there first. Smell is the only sense wired straight into the parts of the brain that handle memory and emotion, without the polite detour the other senses take through reason. A skyline is admired; a smell is felt before it is understood. So while my eyes were busy being tourists — checking the famous tower against the photographs — my sense of smell was doing the deeper, dumber work of learning the city by heart. Although the postcards sell the skyline, what actually lodges in memory is the particular sourness of a stairwell, the wet-stone breath of an underpass, the frying oil of a corner I passed each evening without ever looking up.

What the nose notices is precisely what the eye is trained to overlook. A skyline is built to be seen; it is curated, lit, and sold. A smell is never staged for the visitor. It leaks out of kitchens and drains and markets that no one decorated for arrival, and so it tells a truer story. It is not the monument but the morning smell of a particular bakery that finally tells me I have stopped being a visitor. The monument was always going to be there for anyone; the bakery became mine only because I walked past it enough times for its warmth to mean something.

Granted, this can be romanticised, and not every smell is a gift. Much of what a newcomer breathes is simply pollution, poverty, and bad drains, and to call it 'the texture of daily life' from the safety of a short stay is a kind of tourism in itself. The man who must live beside the canal does not find its rot poetic. This objection is honest and worth keeping close: a smell that charms a visitor for a week may be a burden someone else carries for a lifetime, and my delight in the strangeness of a place is partly the luxury of being able to leave it.

Even so, the point survives. The way I came to belong was not by memorising the map but by the day the smells stopped being strange. There comes a morning when the diesel and the fruit-rot and the bread no longer register as 'foreign' but simply as the air, and on that morning, without deciding to, I had begun to live there rather than visit. Belonging, it turns out, is not announced by the eye recognising a landmark. It is confirmed, far more quietly, by the nose ceasing to notice what once it could not ignore — the moment a place stops smelling like somewhere else and starts smelling like home.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentences to elevate the overlooked detail
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What the nose notices is precisely what the eye is trained to overlook' (what-cleft) và 'It is not the monument but the morning smell of a particular bakery that finally tells me …' (it-cleft) dùng cấu trúc chẻ câu để nâng một chi tiết tầm thường (mùi hương) lên trên cái biểu tượng được kỳ vọng (đài tưởng niệm).
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What the nose notices is precisely what the eye is trained to overlook' (what-cleft) and 'It is not the monument but the morning smell of a particular bakery that finally tells me …' (it-cleft) use clefts to raise a humble detail (a smell) above the expected icon (the monument).

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Concession + passive to contrast the curated with the leaked
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Although the postcards sell the skyline, …' nhượng bộ bằng 'although', còn các câu bị động 'A skyline is built to be seen; it is curated, lit, and sold' so với 'A smell is never staged for the visitor' đặt cái được dàn dựng cạnh cái rỉ ra một cách tự nhiên.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Although the postcards sell the skyline, …' concedes with 'although', while the passives 'A skyline is built to be seen; it is curated, lit, and sold' against 'A smell is never staged for the visitor' set the curated beside the naturally leaked.

sampleTranslationVi:
Tôi đến thành phố vào ban đêm, và suốt ba ngày đầu tôi không thể vẽ nổi đường chân trời của nó dù mạng sống có phụ thuộc vào điều đó. Vậy mà tôi đã có thể mô tả được mùi của nó. Rất lâu trước khi tôi có thể gọi tên một con phố, thành phố đã tự giới thiệu mình qua mũi: mùi dầu diesel ấm gần nhà ga, một mùi thối ngọt của trái cây bên bờ kênh, và, mỗi sáng lúc sáu giờ, mùi bánh mì từ một lò bánh mà phải một tuần sau tôi mới tìm ra. Con mắt thì choáng ngợp và vô dụng; cái mũi thì đã bắt tay vào việc, phân loại, so sánh, và lặng lẽ quyết định đây là loại nơi chốn gì.

Có một lý do khiến cái mũi đến đó trước. Khứu giác là giác quan duy nhất được nối thẳng vào những phần não xử lý ký ức và cảm xúc, mà không qua cái lối vòng lịch sự mà các giác quan khác phải đi qua lý trí. Một đường chân trời được chiêm ngưỡng; một mùi hương được cảm nhận trước khi được hiểu. Thế nên trong khi đôi mắt tôi bận rộn làm khách du lịch — đối chiếu cái tháp nổi tiếng với những tấm ảnh — khứu giác của tôi đang làm cái công việc sâu hơn, đần hơn là học thuộc lòng thành phố. Dù những tấm bưu thiếp bán đường chân trời, cái thực sự đọng lại trong ký ức là mùi chua đặc trưng của một cầu thang bộ, hơi thở ẩm mùi đá của một đường hầm đi bộ, mùi dầu chiên của một góc phố tôi đi qua mỗi tối mà chẳng bao giờ ngước nhìn lên.

Cái mà mũi nhận ra chính xác là cái mà mắt đã được huấn luyện để bỏ qua. Một đường chân trời được dựng nên để được ngắm; nó được sắp đặt, chiếu sáng và rao bán. Một mùi hương chẳng bao giờ được dàn dựng cho khách tham quan. Nó rỉ ra từ những căn bếp, những cống rãnh và những khu chợ mà chẳng ai trang trí để đón khách, và vì thế nó kể một câu chuyện thật hơn. Không phải đài tưởng niệm mà chính mùi bánh buổi sáng của một lò bánh cụ thể mới rốt cuộc bảo tôi rằng tôi đã thôi làm một khách tham quan. Đài tưởng niệm thì lúc nào cũng sẽ ở đó cho bất kỳ ai; lò bánh ấy trở thành của tôi chỉ vì tôi đã đi qua nó đủ nhiều lần để hơi ấm của nó có ý nghĩa gì đó.

Công nhận, điều này có thể bị lãng mạn hóa, và không phải mùi nào cũng là một món quà. Phần lớn cái mà một người mới đến hít thở chỉ đơn giản là ô nhiễm, sự nghèo khổ và những cống rãnh tệ hại, và gọi nó là 'chất liệu của đời sống thường nhật' từ sự an toàn của một kỳ lưu trú ngắn ngủi tự nó cũng là một dạng du lịch. Người đàn ông phải sống bên bờ kênh không thấy cái mùi thối của nó nên thơ gì cả. Phản đối này thành thật và đáng giữ cho gần: một mùi hương làm say đắm một du khách trong một tuần có thể là một gánh nặng mà người khác mang theo cả đời, và niềm vui của tôi trước sự lạ lẫm của một nơi chốn một phần là cái xa xỉ của việc có thể rời khỏi nó.

Dẫu vậy, luận điểm vẫn sống sót. Cách tôi dần thuộc về nơi đó không phải bằng việc học thuộc bản đồ mà bằng cái ngày những mùi hương thôi còn lạ lẫm. Có một buổi sáng khi mùi dầu diesel, mùi trái cây thối và mùi bánh mì không còn được ghi nhận như 'ngoại lai' nữa mà đơn giản chỉ là không khí, và vào buổi sáng ấy, mà chẳng hề quyết định, tôi đã bắt đầu sống ở đó thay vì ghé thăm. Hóa ra, sự thuộc về không được tuyên bố bởi việc con mắt nhận ra một địa danh. Nó được xác nhận, một cách thầm lặng hơn nhiều, bởi việc cái mũi thôi không còn nhận ra cái mà có một thời nó không thể phớt lờ — khoảnh khắc một nơi chốn thôi không còn mùi của một chỗ nào khác và bắt đầu có mùi của nhà.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a what-cleft to elevate the overlooked detail?
options: What the nose notices is precisely what the eye is trained to overlook.
The nose notices what the eye overlooks precisely, which is the training of it.
Noticing the eye is what the nose trained to overlook precisely does.
Trained to overlook the eye is what precisely the nose notices what.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What the nose notices is …' is a what-cleft foregrounding the detail the eye ignores.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'What X notices is …' frame.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an it-cleft to put the bakery above the monument?
options: The monument tells me less than the bakery smell which I stopped being a visitor.
It is not the monument but the morning smell of a particular bakery that finally tells me I have stopped being a visitor.
Telling me I stopped is what the monument the bakery smell does visitor.
A visitor I stopped being the bakery monument it is not but that smell tells finally.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is not X but Y that …' is an it-cleft raising the bakery smell over the monument.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'It is not X but Y that …' frame.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive to describe how a skyline is presented?
options: The city builds the skyline to see it and they curate and light and sell it.
Building the skyline is what the city to be seen curated does sold.
A skyline is built to be seen; it is curated, lit, and sold.
Sold and lit curated to be seen is the skyline built it the city by a.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'is built … is curated, lit, and sold' uses passives to show the skyline is staged for viewing.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option chains passive verbs without naming who acts?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to evoke a memory' mean?
options: To erase a memory deliberately
To write a memory down
To doubt whether a memory is real
To bring a memory vividly to mind
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Evoke a memory' means to call it up, as smell does so directly in the essay.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of a smell suddenly bringing back the past.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence concedes the objection most honestly?
options: Granted, this can be romanticised, and not every smell is a gift; much of it is pollution and bad drains.
Anyone who dislikes a city's smell simply has no imagination.
Obviously every smell in every city is beautiful and meaningful.
People who live beside canals should be grateful for the poetry.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B honestly concedes that romanticising a place's smells ignores who suffers them.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option admits the writer may be romanticising?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option hedges the writer's own position appropriately?
options: My delight in a place proves I understand it better than the locals do.
My delight in the strangeness of a place is partly the luxury of being able to leave it.
My delight has nothing to do with my being a temporary visitor.
Delight strangeness luxury leave locals understand better always partly.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'is partly the luxury of being able to leave it' acknowledges the limits of the visitor's view.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option that admits the writer's view is a privilege.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, why does the nose register a city before the eye?
options: Because smells are louder than sights
Because the eye is closed at night
Because smell is wired straight into memory and emotion, skipping the detour through reason
Because cities have no skyline at first
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says smell connects directly to memory and emotion without the reasoning detour.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps a consistent reflective register?
options: Honestly the city smelled super weird at first but you get used to it, right?
Basically the place just stank and then it didn't, simple as that.
It's kind of strange how, like, you remember smells more than buildings, you know?
A skyline is admired; a smell is felt before it is understood.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B is precise and reflective; the others slip into casual speech.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots from the objection back to the reflection?
options: Even so, the point survives: the way I came to belong was the day the smells stopped being strange.
Anyway, smells never meant anything to me at all.
In conclusion, all city smells are simply unpleasant.
On the contrary, I never grew used to anything there.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, the point survives …' accepts the objection and returns to the essay's reflection on belonging.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase accepts the prior point and reasserts the reflection?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to find one's bearings' mean?
options: To lose all sense of direction permanently
To work out where you are and orient yourself
To buy a map of a city
To complain about being lost
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Find one's bearings' means getting oriented in an unfamiliar place.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of figuring out which way is which.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses parallel structure most effectively?
options: sorting, to compare, and it quietly decided what place this was kind of
to sort, the comparing, and quietly deciding what kind place this was of
sorting, comparing, and quietly deciding what kind of place this was
sort, comparing quietly, and to decide this place what kind of was
mcQuestions.explanation: Three matched '-ing' verbs form a clean parallel describing the nose's work.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps all three verbs in the same '-ing' form?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central reflection about belonging?
options: That belonging is achieved by memorising a city's map
That belonging requires recognising the famous skyline
That a visitor can never belong to a foreign city
That belonging is confirmed quietly, by the nose ceasing to notice smells that once seemed foreign
mcQuestions.explanation: The conclusion locates belonging in the moment the strange smells become simply 'the air'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to feel out of place' mean here?
options: To feel that one does not belong or fit in
To feel physically ill
To feel lost on a map
To dislike a smell strongly
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Feel out of place' means sensing you do not belong, the newcomer's early state.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of not fitting in somewhere.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best captures the essay's nuanced conclusion?
options: Belonging is announced loudly the moment you recognise a landmark.
Belonging is confirmed quietly, the moment a place stops smelling like somewhere else and starts smelling like home.
A city's smell is the only thing that ever matters about it.
No one can ever truly belong to a place they were not born in.
mcQuestions.explanation: The conclusion ties belonging to the nose no longer registering the once-foreign smells.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option about a place starting to smell like home.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an em-dash insertion correctly to add a parenthetical action?
options: while my eyes — busy being tourists checking — the famous tower photographs my sense smell learning by heart.
my sense of smell learning — while my eyes busy — the famous tower tourists by heart the city were.
while my eyes were busy being tourists — checking the famous tower against the photographs — my sense of smell was learning the city by heart
checking the tower — while my eyes were — busy photographs my sense of smell the city against learning.
mcQuestions.explanation: The dashes enclose the aside; remove them and 'while my eyes were busy being tourists … my sense of smell was learning the city by heart' still reads.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option lets you delete the dashed aside and keep a full sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to grow on someone' mean?
options: To become physically attached to a person
To grow taller than someone
To irritate someone slowly
To become gradually more likeable or familiar over time
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Grow on someone' means a place or thing becomes more appealing the longer one knows it.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of liking something more the longer you live with it.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a smell that instantly returns you to a particular place or period of your life. Why does that smell carry the memory so completely when a photograph of the same place leaves you cold — and what does that say about which sense is really doing the work of remembering?
